segments.s288-g33: که at the start is a subordinating conjunction 'that' linking this clause to the surrounding narrative. دانا is used as a noun 'the wise (man/sage)' acting as the agent. زد is a past tense verb here used idiomatically (poetic) meaning 'recited/told' or 'sounded' (rather than literal 'hit'); it follows the subject in this poetic word order. این داستان بزرگ is the direct object phrase 'this great tale' with the demonstrative این before the noun داستان and the adjective بزرگ post‑nominal (typical flexible adjective placement in Persian poetry).
segments.s288-g34: The initial که again introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that'. شیری (here with the adjectival form meaning 'lion‑like' used substantively) = 'a lion'. The second که is the relative 'which/who'. بگریزد is a present/subjunctive‑form (classical/poetic present) meaning 'flees/escapes' and governs the following prepositional phrase از چنگ گرگ ('from the wolf's claw'); از is the preposition 'from', چنگ is 'claw/talon' and گرگ is 'wolf'. The clause is left unfinished (ellipsis) because the thought continues into the next sentence.
segments.s288-g35: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction meaning 'it must not be that / one ought not to' and introduces a subordinate clause expressing prohibition or inevitability. گرگ is the subject 'the wolf'. از پسش is a prepositional phrase 'from behind him / from its rear' (پسش = 'behind it/him'). در before کشد is the prepositional/adverbial element 'in/into' here fused with the verb; کشد is the finite verb (classical poetic form of کشیدن) meaning 'pull/pursue/drag' in context 'pursue/drag after' — the precise lexical sense in the supplied list did not match exactly, so treat کشد here as a verbal 'pursue/drag'.
segments.s288-g36: که again links the subordinate reason/explanation clause 'for/that'. او را uses او 'he' plus the object marker را. همان bخت خود is a noun phrase 'that very fortune of his' (همان = 'the same/that very', بخت = 'fate/destiny', خود = possessive/emphatic 'his own'). برکشد is a poetic compound verb (بر + کشد) meaning here 'will carry/take/bring upon him' — i.e. 'that same fate will take him' (metaphorical 'take his life' or 'overtake him'). Grammatically this is a third‑person present/future sense in narrative (classical usage).
segments.s288-g37: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase 'what (means/resources/effort)' or 'however (with whatever resources)'. بپیوند here is a classical/poetic past or perfective sense 'he joined/was joined' (the b‑prefix is the classical present/imperfective marker but in narrative functions as simple past/gnomic); in context it means 'he attached himself / joined (in action)'. و is the conjunction 'and'. چندی is an adverb 'for a while' (meaning 'some time'), and شتافت is a past tense verb 'hastened/rushed'. The sequence describes an action and its short duration.
segments.s288-g38: کس here is used in the negative sense 'no one'. از روز بد literally 'from a bad day' = 'from ill fortune' (روز = 'day/time/occasion', بد = 'bad/unfavorable'). هم functions as 'even' or emphatic 'indeed', and رهایی is the noun 'escape/freedom'. نیافت is the past negative of یافتن 'to find/obtain' meaning 'did not obtain/was not able to find' — together meaning 'no one even found escape from ill fortune' (i.e. no one escaped misfortune).
segments.s288-g39: هلا is an interjection/exhortation here ('come on!' or 'quick!'). زود is an adverb 'quick/soon', and بشتاب is an imperative 'hurry!' (second person command). کآمد is a compact classical form 'it came' (here introducing the event), and سپاه is the subject 'the army/host'. The clause is an abrupt call to haste followed by the narrative note that the army arrived.
segments.s288-g40: از ایران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (از = 'from', ایران = 'Iran/land of the Iranians'). و links the next action. بر ما here means 'toward us / against us' (بر with dative/target meaning 'toward/on us'), ما = 'us'. گرفتند is past tense 'they took/seized' and governs راه 'road/way' — گرفتند راه = 'they took the road' or 'they closed off the way' toward us; راه is the object 'path/road'.
segments.s288-g41: نشستند is a past (perfective) 3rd-person-plural verb meaning ‘they sat/settled’. Here it appears before its complement, a common poetic inversion. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’ taking the following noun bāreh as its object; هر دو سوار is a unit meaning ‘both riders’ (هر + دو + سوار ⇒ ‘each of the two riders’ / ‘both riders’). Note: the lemma given for نشستند in the data list is not the usual نشستن, so its sense was not available in the provided list.
segments.s288-g42: کشیدند is the past 3rd-person-plural of کشیدن, here meaning ‘they moved/drew (away)’; Persian allows the subject or descriptive noun (پویان) to follow the verb, so پویان (‘the swift/active ones’) functions as the agent/subject. از آن مرغزار is a prepositional phrase ‘from that meadow’ marking origin.
segments.s288-g43: ز بیشه به بالا is an adverbial phrase: ز + بیشه = ‘out of/from the thicket’, به بالا = ‘upwards/toward the height’. نهادند is the past 3rd-plural of نهادن ‘to set/put’ used idiomatically with روی (‘to set/turn one’s face’). روی (face) is the object whose direction is being set — literally ‘they set/turned (their) face upward from the grove’.
segments.s288-g44: This is a compact, poetic nominal cluster of epithets describing the fighters. The line strings adjectives/nouns without an explicit verb: خونی (‘blood-stained/bloody’) modifies the following noun دلاور (‘brave, valiant’). Then the phrase repeats with دو (numeric) and پرخاشجوی (‘aggressive, quarrelsome’). In Persian poetry such adjectival clusters are appositive attributes linked to the preceding action (they turned their faces toward these men).
segments.s288-g45: به هامون is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into the plain’ (به + هامون). کشیدند is past 3rd-plural of کشیدن meaning here ‘they moved/rode (toward)’. هر دو سوار (‘each of the two riders’ = ‘both riders’) functions as the subject; word order places the verb before the subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s288-g46: پراندیشه is an adjective/participial descriptor ‘full of thought / anxious’. تا introduces the subordinate phrase indicating extent or concern (‘as to / about’), and چون here functions as an interrogative/relativizer ‘how’. بسیچند modifies کار and means ‘many/several’; کار (‘deeds, affairs’) is the nominal object under consideration. The whole clause means they were pensive about how many deeds (matters) there were.
segments.s288-g47: پدید آمد is a compound verb meaning ‘appeared’ (پدید + آمد, past 3rd-singular). از دور is an adverbial ‘from afar’. پس is a connective ‘then/thereupon’. گستهم is a 1st-person past form meaning ‘I wandered/turned/moved’ (poetic/archaic first-person), so the clause shifts to the speaker’s action after the appearance.
segments.s288-g48: ندیدند is past 3rd-plural of دیدن with negation prefix ن- here ‘they did not see’. با او is the prepositional phrase ‘with him’ (i.e., accompanying him). سواری is the noun ‘rider/horseman’ (object), and به هم (‘together/with one another’) stresses accompaniment or company; the whole means ‘they did not see any rider accompanying him.’
segments.s288-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar' (literal 'from the distance'), and لهاک و فرشیدورد are two names placed after that phrase; word order is typical poetic (circumstance → named actors).
segments.s288-g2: گذشتند is the simple past plural 'they passed/they departed.' پویان here functions as an epithet/description ('the moving/active ones' or 'the company') placed after the verb for poetic inversion. ز دشت نبرد is a poetic locative/partitive phrase 'from the plain of battle' (ز = from/of, دشت نبرد = battlefield/plain of battle).
segments.s288-g3: به یک ساعت here marks a temporal/durational span 'for one hour' (به + duration). از هفت فرسنگ راه is a partitive/genitive phrase 'of the seven‑farsang road' — i.e. a portion or section of a road described as seven farsangs long; poetic ordering places the measure before the road phrase.
segments.s288-g4: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/they departed' (بر- prefix giving a sense of onward/off). ایمن is an adjective 'safe' used predicatively; ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army of Iran' with ز = 'from' and سپاه = 'army.' The adjective + z‑phrase expresses 'safe from the Iranian army.'
segments.s288-g5: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' modifying بیشه ('a certain grove'). دیدند is the simple past 'they saw.' و links to آب روان, where آب is 'water' and روان is an adjective/epithet 'flowing/running,' giving 'a running stream.'
segments.s288-g6: بدو is 'to it' (to that place/thing) rather than an imperative here; اندرون = 'inside/within.' سایه is 'shade' and کاروان is the subject 'the caravan.' The verb is elided (common in Persian poetry) and understood from context: 'the caravan went into its shade.'
segments.s288-g7: به بیشه درون combines به + درون to mark motion/position 'within/into the grove' (emphatic locative). The list مرغ و نخچیر و شیر enumerates inhabitants: 'birds, game (prey), and lions' — simple nominal apposition separated by و.
segments.s288-g8: درخت از بر is an idiomatic spatial phrase 'trees on every side/around' (از بر = around/on the side), followed by و سبزه و آب زیر 'and green growth (grass) and water beneath/underneath' — سبزه = greenery, آب زیر = 'water below.' The nouns follow one another in poetic listing, with زیر indicating 'beneath.'
segments.s288-g49: دلیران (subject, plural) + چو (temporal 'when') introduces a subordinate temporal clause. سر را is a definite object (سر 'head' + را object marker), and برافراختند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they raised/erected' that completes the clause; word order is Subject – temporal marker – object – verb (typical of older Persian poetry).
segments.s288-g50: This is a second clause: چو + دیدند ('when they saw') forms another temporal clause, and بشناختند is the main past verb 'they recognized'. او را is the definite direct object 'him'. مر is an archaic/obscure particle here (not covered by the listed senses) placed before او را for emphasis or archaic rhythm.
segments.s288-g51: گرفتند is a 3rd-person plural past 'they took/seized' used idiomatically with گفت و گوی to mean 'they entered into conversation'. یک با دگر ('one with another') expresses mutuality/each other; گفت و گوی is the noun 'talk/conversation' (گفت و گو). The verb precedes the object phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s288-g52: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). یک تن is 'one person/man' (تن = person), سوی ما نهادست روی is the predicate: سوی = 'toward', ما = 'us', نهادست is the perfect/past participial sense 'has placed/set' (here idiomatically 'has turned/set his face'), and روی = 'face' — together meaning 'has set his face toward us'. Word order is Subject (یک تن) + adverbial (سوی ما) + verb (نهادست) + object/compl. (روی) in this poetic arrangement.
segments.s288-g53: نیابد is negative of یابد ('he finds'); in older/poetic Persian the present/subjunctive form carries a future/irrealis sense here ('he will not find'). رهایی = 'escape/release' is the object, ز ما = 'from us'. گستهم in this context is a proper name (the hero Gostaham) and is placed after the predicate for emphasis/rhythm; that proper-name sense is not in the provided list.
segments.s288-g54: مگر introduces an exception ('unless'). بخت بد = 'ill fortune/fate' (بخت = fate, بد = bad). The verbal complex کرد خواهد ستم is a poetic/flexible order meaning 'will do/bring injustice' — کرد (to do/make) with the auxiliary خواهد ('will') and the noun ستم ('injustice') as the action; overall: 'unless ill fate should inflict injustice.' This clause expresses the only condition under which escape would be possible.
segments.s288-g55: جز از introduces an exception 'except from/for'. گستهم here is the proper name Gostaham (not one of the provided senses). نیست کامد is an older poetic negative-existential construction meaning 'does not come / will not come' (نیست + کامد), followed by به جنگ 'to the battle' — so the clause means 'no one will come to the battle except Gostaham.'
segments.s288-g56: درفش دلیران is a genitive noun phrase 'the banner of the brave'. گرفته is a perfect/past participle 'taken/seized' (implicitly: 'they have taken'). به چنگ is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'in (their) grasp / by the claw' (چنگ 'claw' used idiomatically for a firm grip). The implied subject is the people/warriors; the phrase describes that they have seized the banner.
segments.s288-g89: بیامد (بـِ + آمد) is a classical/perhaps slightly archaic perfect/past narrative form meaning “he came” or “he arrived.” خمیده is an adjectival participle ‘bent’ describing posture; به + زین اندرون places that posture spatially: به (toward/into) governs زین (saddle) and اندرون (inside), so the phrase means he came bent down into/into the interior of the saddle.
segments.s288-g90: همی before a past-tense verb is a poetic/progressive particle indicating continued or habitual action (here: he kept doing). راند is the past ‘drove/rode (it)’ with اسب as its object (object following verb is normal in Persian poetry). و links to the next clause; the second همی + ریخت uses the same progressive sense to say that blood kept pouring/flowing (ریختن here = to pour/spill).
segments.s288-g91: و is the connective 'and.' زآنجا = 'from there/thence' gives the point of departure; سوی + noun is a directional phrase 'toward' or 'to.' چشمه‌ساری is a locative noun 'a spring/streamlet area.' رسید is the past 'reached/arrived.' Word order is poetic (zānjā + suyi + destination + رسید = 'from there toward the spring he arrived').
segments.s288-g92: هم ... و هم ... is the correlative structure 'both ... and ...'. آبِ روان is a noun phrase in which آب 'water' is qualified by روان 'running/flowing' (here روان = flowing). دید is the past 'he saw' and appears twice, once for each coordinate. The order (object before verb) is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s288-g93: فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'he alighted / came down' (فرود as noun/verb stem + آمد). The following و links the next action. اسب را uses the object marker را to mark 'the horse' as definite object; بر درخت is the locative complement 'to/on a tree' preparing for the verb of tying which follows in the next segment.
segments.s288-g94: ببست is the past 3rd-singular 'he bound/tied' (here transitive). و connects to the final clause. به + آب + اندر آمد = 'he went/entered into the water' (اندر آمد = entered/came into). ز + بخت is a short poetic phrase 'by/through fortune' or 'by luck/fate' (ز used here in the sense 'because of / by').
segments.s288-g95: بخورد is the classical past form meaning 'he drank' (ب- prefix + خورد). آب بسیار is the object 'much water' (بسیار modifies آب). و کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد 'did/uttered' combines with the interjection آفرین to mean 'he cried/uttered “Afarin!”' (i.e. he exclaimed in praise).
segments.s288-g96: ببستش is the past 'he bound/tied' with the enclitic object pronoun -ش ('him/it') attached; this makes the object explicit on the verb. تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' and here opens a parenthetical or reported phrase ('you say' / 'you have said'); گفتی would normally be a past verb 'you said,' but the supplied sense-list for گفتی does not include a simple past-verb reading, so in context it functions as a parenthetical 'you say / as you say.' سراسر زمین is an adverbial phrase 'throughout the earth/land' modifying the preceding clause.
segments.s288-g105: This is a compact nominal cluster: سر (head) governs نامدارانِ توران (the renowned/illustrious men of Turan) forming 'the heads of Turan's famous men.' سپاه (army) is appended as the agent/topic; Persian poetic word order can place these noun groups adjacent so the force (سپاه) that performed the action is signalled here before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s288-g106: ببرد is the verbal form from برد (here 'take/carry') meaning 'took/carried (away)'; the following برد appears as a poetic echo/nominal echo of the same root (often found in epic diction) and can be read as a reinforcement or as the noun 'bord' (spoils/victory) used for emphasis. پیش + بیدار شاه = 'in the presence of / before the awakened king' (پیش + noun = 'before, in front of').
segments.s288-g107: بدان is the imperative formed with the prefix ب- + دان (literally 'know!'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' and is followed by بداند (3rd-person subjunctive 'he may know'). که begins the content clause 'that ...' and جز به نام = 'except for name/fame' (جز + prepositional phrase with به).
segments.s288-g108: نمردم is the negative past-form meaning 'I did not die' (used here idiomatically as a strong negation about dying except for the stated cause). به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in/for the world' (a common epic collocation), همین است کام = 'this is the (very) desire/wish' — کام here denotes 'desire' and یہی (همین) points to this specific thing (seeking name) as the world's desire.
segments.s288-g65: این عبارت فشرده‌ی خبری است: 'یکی' (indefinite subject = 'someone'/'one') is placed before the verbal phrase. 'تیر زد' is a simple past (he/it struck/shot). The prepositional phrase 'بر سرش' ('on/against his head') follows the verb; the proper name 'گستهم' (Gostaham) appears at the end as the person affected. Word order is typical of epic Persian (subject/indefinite + verb + prepositional object + name).
segments.s288-g66: 'که' introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that' / 'such that'). 'با خون' ('with blood') gives the instrument/medium. 'برآمیخت' is a past-form describing the mixing (here effectively passive/severe result: 'was/ became mixed'). 'مغزش' ('his brain') is the affected noun; 'به هم' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'together/into pieces' — the whole clause describes the grisly result of the wound.
segments.s288-g67: 'نگون گشت' is a compound past indicating downfall/being overthrown ('fell'). 'و هم' connects the next action ('and also/at the same time'). 'در زمان' is a temporal phrase 'at the moment/instant'; 'جان بداد' is an archaic past construction meaning 'gave up his life' (literally 'gave life' → 'died'). Note that 'بداد' here is used as a past tense verb, an older poetic form.
segments.s288-g68: Here 'شد' is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was' used to assert the state/result. 'آن نامور' = 'that famed one' is a noun phrase identifying the fallen person. 'گرد ویسه نژاد' is an epithet: 'گرد' in classical usage here functions as part of a heroic epithet meaning roughly 'hero/man' (i.e. 'the man of Viseh's line'); 'ویسه نژاد' = 'of Viseh's race/lineage.' The phrase as a whole states that that famed scion of Viseh's lineage was (thus).
segments.s288-g69: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'لهاک' is the (proper) subject name; 'روی برادر' ('the face of his brother') is the direct object phrase (چسبیدهٔ 'روی' + possessor implied); 'بدید' is the simple past 'saw.' Word order is Subject – (object) – Verb in a compact poetic line.
segments.s288-g70: 'بدانست' is the past tense 'he knew/realized' and introduces the content clause. 'کز' is the classical connective 'that' / 'which' introducing the subordinate clause. 'کارزار' = 'battle/field' and 'آرمید' (past of 'آرمیدن') literally 'rested' but in this context poetically means 'ceased fighting/was laid to rest' (i.e., was dead). Together the subordinate clause reports what he realized on seeing his brother.
segments.s288-g71: 'بلرزید' is an intransitive past 'he trembled/shook.' 'وز' here is an archaic preposition meaning 'from' (classical variant of 'از') introducing cause; 'درد او' = 'his pain' is the cause. 'خیره شد' = 'became stunned/dazed' (خیره = dazed/staring), so the clause describes physical shock: he trembled and, from the pain, was stunned.
segments.s288-g72: This is a simple clause: 'جهان' (the world/life) is the subject, 'پیش چشم‌اندرش' is a frozen prepositional phrase meaning 'before his eyes' (چشم‌اندرش = 'in front of his eyes'), and 'تیره شد' is the compound predicate 'grew dark/was darkened.' The effect clause states the subjective perception after the wound.
segments.s288-g9: به + verbal noun (نخچیر کردن) expresses purpose: 'for/to hunt.' نخچیر here is a verbal-noun meaning 'game/prey' used to form the action 'نخچیر کردن' (hunt) with کردن as the light verb. فرود آمدند is a simple past (3rd person plural) meaning 'they descended/ alighted,' so the whole clause reads 'they descended for hunting.'
segments.s288-g10: وز آن تشنگی is an archaic/poetic clause meaning 'from/ because of that thirst' (وز here functions like از). سوی رود آمدند is the verb phrase 'they came toward the river' (آمدند past plural) with سوی marking direction.
segments.s288-g11: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause 'when the water came in' (چو = 'when', آب = 'water', اندر آمد = 'came into/rose'). ببایست functions impersonally/obligatively here ('it was necessary' / 'one must'), followed by the noun نان ('bread') as the required thing: 'bread was needed.'
segments.s288-g12: به اندوه و شادی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in sorrow and (in) joy.' نبندد is an older-form verb (negative + present/subjunctive third-person) meaning 'does not/ should not close' and دهان is the thing that does not close: 'the mouth does not/should not be closed.' The clause states a general maxim or observation.
segments.s288-g13: بگشتند is the simple past (3rd person plural) 'they returned/turned back.' بر گرد (compound preposition) + آن مرغزار = 'around that meadow' (word order: verb then locative phrase).
segments.s288-g14: فگندند (past plural) 'they threw/cast down' takes the following noun phrase as its object: بسیار مایه شکار = 'a great quantity/resource of prey' (بسیار = 'many/great', مایه = 'resource/stock', شکار = 'prey'). The clause means they laid out or scattered much game.
segments.s288-g15: برافروختند آتش is verb + object in past plural: 'they kindled a fire.' زان is an archaic contracted preposition 'from that/thereof' referring back to the fire or its result, and کباب is the roasted meat derived from that fire: 'and from that, (the) kebab.'
segments.s288-g16: بخوردند here means 'they ate' (past plural). و کردند سر سوی خواب is an idiomatic sequence: کردند (they made/did) + سر (head) + سوی خواب (toward sleep) = 'they turned their heads toward sleep' (i.e., they went to sleep). The verb کردند supports the motion of 'سر' toward 'خواب.'
segments.s288-g73: ز + noun(+possessive) marks source/reason in classical Persian; here ز روشن‌روانش = “from/with his روشن‌روان (his clear/bright soul)”. به سیری رسید is an idiomatic verb phrase: به + noun + رسید (past of رسیدن) = “reached to/attained [satiation]”; رسید is 3rd person singular past.
segments.s288-g74: کمان را uses the object marker را to mark definite object. به زه کرد uses کردن (here 'to make/put') with به + instrument (زه = bowstring) to mean “strung the bow”; this is a light-verb construction. و اندر کشید is a coordinated past clause: اندر is a classical locative/poetic particle and کشید (past of کشیدن) = “he drew/pulled (it)”.
segments.s288-g75: شدند is the 3rd person plural past of شدن used as a copula/verb of becoming. آن زمان is a temporal adverbial (“at that time/then”). خسته is the predicate adjective “tired/weary”, and هر دو سوار (“both riders”) is the (postposed) subject — classical word order allows verb + time + predicate + subject.
segments.s288-g76: به شمشیر is an instrumental prepositional phrase (“with swords”). برساختند is past plural of برساختن (here ‘made/raised/engaged in’), and کارزار is the object/noun ‘battle’ — literal order: instrument → verb → object, a typical poetic order meaning they brandished swords and engaged in battle.
segments.s288-g77: یکایک = “one by one/each”; بر + او marks direction/target (“against/toward him”). گستهم here functions as a poetic participial/adjectival idea (outstretched/extended) modifying دست; دست یافت uses یافت (past of یافتن) in the sense “reached/laid hold of” — literally “one by one their outstretched hands reached him.”
segments.s288-g78: ز here = ‘from/because of’ (classical preposition). کینه is the noun ‘hatred/grudge’; چنان is an adverb ‘so/such’; خسته is ‘weary/exhausted’. اندر شتافت: اندر is a classical particle and شتافت is past of شتافتن ‘to hasten/rush’ — together = “he rushed in” or “he hastened upon [them]”, with the preceding phrase giving motive/condition.
segments.s288-g79: به گردنش = to/at his neck (به + noun + possessive ۀ/ش = ‘to his neck’). بر زد is past tense ‘struck upon’ (زد = struck), and یکی تیغ تیز is an indefinite noun phrase ‘a certain sharp sword’ (one + sword + adjective). Word order is poetic but means ‘a sharp sword struck his neck.’
segments.s288-g80: برآورد here is used in a classical/poetic sense ‘brought forth/caused to arise’ (an intransitive or middle use is common in poetry). ناگاه is the adverb ‘suddenly’. زو is a contracted classical form (ز + او) meaning ‘from him/it’. رستخیز is a noun meaning ‘uprising/resurrection’ — so the clause says that suddenly an uprising/resurrection arose from him/it.
segments.s288-g25: نوند اسب او is an appositional phrase: نوند is a proper name placed before اسب او (‘his horse’) — ‘Navand, his horse’. بوی اسبان is the object phrase ‘the scent of horses’ (بوی + genitive اسبان). شنید is simple past 3rd sg. The verb is used here in a slightly poetic sense (animals 'hear' a scent), so literally ‘heard/caught the scent’.
segments.s288-g26: خروشی برآورد uses برآوردن in the sense ‘to emit/raise’ (here: ‘let out a cry’). و links the two actions. اندر is a poetic preposition/adverb meaning roughly ‘then’/‘thereupon’ (linking the next action), and دمید is past 3rd sg. meaning ‘blew’/‘breathed’ — applied to the horse as a snort.
segments.s288-g27: سبک (adjective) precedes اسب, giving ‘nimble/light horse’. اسب لهاک is a possessive/genitive construction ‘Lahak’s horse’; هم adds ‘also/even’. ز این نشان is a prepositional phrase ‘from/at this sign’ that sets the cause for what follows.
segments.s288-g28: خروشی برآورد repeats the idiom ‘let out a cry’ (برآوردن = ‘to emit/utter’ here). چون introduces a comparison ‘as/like’, and بیهشان is archaic bi- ‘without’ + -شان ‘them’ → ‘as if without them’ (an old-fashioned/compressed construction meaning ‘bereft of them’).
segments.s288-g29: سوی is the preposition ‘toward’; لهاک فرشیدورد is the personal name in the oblique (object of سوی). دمان here functions adverbially (‘at once/straightaway’) in the narrative, but that particular sense is not among the listed options for the word form.
segments.s288-g30: ز خواب خوش is the prepositional phrase ‘from (his) pleasant sleep’ (خوش modifying خواب). آمدش is a past form ‘came to him’ (آمد + enclitic -ش = ‘to him’), and بیدار کرد is the transitive past ‘(he) made (him) awake’ = ‘woke him’ (کرد as causative/auxiliary). Together they mean ‘he came and woke him from his sweet sleep.’
segments.s288-g31: بدو گفت is ‘he said to him’: بدو is dative ‘to him’, and گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ (note: the provided senses list for گفت does not show the verb sense). برخیز is an imperative ‘rise/stand up’. ز این خواب خوش is the prepositional phrase ‘from this pleasant sleep’.
segments.s288-g32: به مردی is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘with manliness’ or ‘like a man’ (به + noun of manner). سر بخت خود را is the direct object: سر بخت ‘the head of (your) fortune’ (an idiomatic image), with خود ‘yourself/your own’ and را marking the definite object. بکش is the imperative of کشیدن; here it means ‘seize/drag/pull’ in an idiomatic sense ‘take hold of/secure your fate’ (not literal killing).
segments.s288-g57: گریزان here is an adjectival/participial complement meaning 'fleeing' and combines with the light-verb phrase بباید شد ('one must become / one must be') to express obligation: 'one must flee.' از پیش + pronoun اوی ('from before him') is the prepositional phrase indicating source/avoidance; اوی is the older oblique form of او used as the object of the preposition.
segments.s288-g58: مگر introduces an exception ('unless'). کاندر is an archaic fused form meaning roughly 'that into / in which' and governs the following verb آرد ('he brings/turns'). The phrase آرد بدین دشت روی literally 'brings (his) face to this plain' is the idiom روی آوردن (to turn one's face/toward), where بدین = به + این 'to this' and روی = 'face'. Note the older word order: verb (آرد) precedes the directional phrase.
segments.s288-g59: وز آنجا functions as 'and/from there' in classical usage (an older conjunctive/locative), followed by به هامون 'toward the Hamun.' نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place/set' used with روی ('face') to mean 'they set their face (toward)' = 'they turned toward.' The object (روی) follows the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s288-g60: پس 'then' gives sequence; اندر دمان 'in the ḏamān' (here 'foothill/skirt of the plain') marks the location. گستهم is the proper name Gostaham (the supplied sense list does not include the name sense), and کینه‌جوی is a postpositive epithet 'revenge-seeking/vengeful.' The copula/verb is omitted (a common epic poetic compression), so the line reads 'then in the foothill was Gostaham, the vengeance-seeker.'
segments.s288-g61: بیامد is a simple past 'he came.' The temporal clause introduced by چو + رسید 'when he reached/approached' ('چو نزدیک ایشان رسید' = 'when he came near them') follows, so the sequence is: he came; when he drew near to them.... Note چر/چو used as the conjunction 'when' here.
segments.s288-g62: چو here introduces a simile ('like/as'): 'چو شیر ژیان' = 'like a living/ferocious lion.' نعره‌ای برکشید is a compact clause: نعره‌ای 'a cry' (indefinite object) + برکشید 'he raised/uttered' (past tense 3rd sing.). The verb is transitive with the cry as its object; the simile modifies how he cried.
segments.s288-g63: بر ایشان is the prepositional phrase 'upon them.' The verb ببارید ('it rained/fell') is used impersonally or with the projectile as subject; تیر خدنگ ('dagger-arrow') is the actor that 'rained down' on them — classical Persian often places the verb before its subject. The compound تیر خدنگ describes the missile (an arrow likened to a dagger).
segments.s288-g64: چو is temporal 'when.' فرشیدورد is a proper name (the sense list gives it as a name). اندر آمد به جنگ literally 'entered into the battle' — اندر آمد is the classical perfect/past 'entered' and به جنگ marks the destination/scene 'into the fight.' Word order again reflects classical placement: name + entered + into battle.
segments.s288-g97: بپیچید (ب‑ + پیچید) is a past, 3rd‑person singular in Ferdowsi's style meaning 'he/it twisted/writhed.' و is the simple conjunction 'and.' غلتید is also a past 3rd‑singular verb 'rolled, tumbled.' بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and precedes its noun phrase. تیره (adjective) modifies خاک and comes before it: 'dark/dusky earth/ground.' The clause literally reads 'he twisted and rolled upon the dark earth.'
segments.s288-g98: سراسر is an adverb/intensifier 'throughout/entirely' that modifies the following noun phrase. همه تن = 'the whole body' (همه qualifies تن). به here marks the instrument/agent of the action and functions like 'by/with' (classical/instrumental usage). شمشیر is 'sword' and چاک is a predicate/adjectival noun 'gash/cleft' (i.e. 'gashed by the sword'). The phrase means 'the whole body was gashed all over by the sword.'
segments.s288-g99: همی گفت is a classical/perfective construction meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he repeatedly said' (همی marks continued or iterative action). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone. روشن is an adjective 'bright/shining' and کردگار is a classical word for 'the Creator/God'; together they form the vocative 'O shining Creator.'
segments.s288-g100: پدید آر is a classical compound imperative: پدید (appear/bring into being) + آر (imperative element 'cause/bring') — together 'bring forth' or 'cause to appear.' زان is a demonstrative 'that/from that' pointing to something previously mentioned; here it means 'that (army).' لشکر نامدار = 'renowned/illustrious army/host.' The clause is an imperative request: 'bring forth that famed host.'
segments.s288-g101: به + دلسوزگی forms a causal adverbial 'out of/by (such) compassion.' Here به marks cause/reason ('because of, out of'). بیژن is the proper name 'Bijan' and گیو را is the object (گیو with the definite object marker را). The verb that would normally follow is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis); the phrase means roughly 'by Bijan's compassion, Giv (was [chosen/spared/sent]).'
segments.s288-g102: وگرنه is the contrastive conditional 'otherwise.' دلاور یکی = 'a certain brave man' (دلاور 'brave/valiant', یکی 'a certain/one'). نیو را names that alternative person (نیو) with the object marker را; again the main verb is omitted and understood from context — 'otherwise a certain hero, Niyu, [would have been].' This is a terse poetic coordinate contrast.
segments.s288-g103: که‌گر is the classical conditional 'that if / whether.' The line uses a compressed poetic antithesis 'گر مرده، گر زنده' (if dead, if alive) expressed here as مرده‌گر زنده — i.e. 'whether dead or alive.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and این جایگاه = 'this place.' Together the phrase means 'whether dead or alive, from this place...' (the condition adverbial).
segments.s288-g104: برد here is the past tense verb 'took/bore' (بردن) in classical narrative: 'carried/took.' مر مرا is an emphatic/object sequence meaning 'me' (the double form is a poetic/emphatic repetition). سوی ایران is the directional phrase 'toward Iran.' سپاه ('the host/army') is the subject: 'the army took me from this place toward Iran.' Word order is typical poetic inversion: directional phrase and subject surround the verb and object.
segments.s288-g81: سرش is the noun سر (head) plus the enclitic pronoun ش = “his,” so “his head.” Word order is subject + locative phrase + verb: زیر پای اندر آمد = “came beneath/under the foot,” where اندر is an archaic/pre-classical preposition meaning ‘in/under.’ چو گوی is a simile tag “like a ball.”
segments.s288-g82: که introduces a relative clause modifying گوی: “which/that …”. آید with the particle همی expresses an ongoing/habitual action (“keeps coming / continually comes”). زخم چوگان is a genitive-like noun phrase “the blow/wound of the polo-mallet,” and بر اوی = بر + اوی (the pronoun ‘him/it’) = “upon him/it.”
segments.s288-g83: چنین است = “thus/so it is.” کردار is the noun “deed/behaviour.” گردان سپهر is a nominal phrase in which گردان is adjectival/participial “revolving” modifying سپهر “the sky/heaven,” so together “the conduct of the revolving heaven.”
segments.s288-g84: ببرد is the present/factual form of بردن meaning “takes/carries away” (here “deprives/takes away”). ز is the poetic preposition “from.” پرورده خویش is “those it has nurtured/raised” (پرورده = nurtured; خوی + ش = its own), and مهر is the object “affection/love.” The sequence is an older word order: verb + ز + source + object.
segments.s288-g85: چو here is conditional “if/when.” سر جوییش is an older phrasing for “if you seek the head” (سر = head; جوییش is a verbal form built on 'جوی' meaning ‘seek’ with a clitic — the provided lemma list for this token does not show the seeking sense, so the exact mapping is irregular). پای یابی نخست = “you will first find the foot” (پای = foot; یابی = “you find” in the 2nd-person present form; نخست = first). The construction places what is sought (سر) before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s288-g86: وگر introduces the contrary conditional “but if.” پای جویی = “if you seek the foot” (جویی is the present/imperative-root form of ‘seek’). سرش پیش تست = “his head is before/in front of you”; سرش = “his head,” پیش = “before/in front of,” and تست is an archaic fused form corresponding to “تو است” (it is before you). (تست as printed is an archaic idiom here, not the modern sense ‘test’.)
segments.s288-g87: به زین بر is a doubled locative: به زین “to/on the saddle” with an additional بر “upon,” an archaic/poetic emphasis meaning ‘upon the saddle.’ چنان خسته بد = “so tired, ill” (چنان = so; خسته = tired; بد = badly/ill), and گستهم is an archaic first‑person verb form “I rode/rode about/I wandered” (here used as the speaker’s action).
segments.s288-g88: که introduces the result clause: “so that …”. بگسست is a perfect/imperfective form meaning “is/would be split/broken apart.” خواهد is the auxiliary “will” (future/intent), so بگسست خواهد = “will break/split.” تو گفتی here functions like “you would say” (an idiomatic comment), and ز هم = “from each other/apart.” The whole clause expresses that the speaker was so exhausted one would think the saddle (or the rider and saddle) would come apart.
segments.s288-g17: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بد روزگار is a compound meaning 'ill/bad fortune' (بد modifies روزگار). دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave', followed by the postpositive adjective دژم 'sullen/grim' — classical Persian allows adjectives after the noun, so the whole cluster means 'when ill fortune (puts) the brave in a sullen state'.
segments.s288-g18: کجا is an interrogative 'where/how' used rhetorically. خواب سازد is a verbal phrase: سازد (from ساختن) in the present/past-narrative form 'makes/causes' and takes خواب 'sleep' as its object. بر ایشان marks the patient/target 'upon them', and ستم 'oppression/injustice' is the agent/topic — 'how can oppression cause sleep upon them?'.
segments.s288-g19: فرو is an adverb 'down/deeply' modifying the past verb خفت 'slept/fell asleep'. The two names لهاک و فرشیدورد form the (compound) subjects of the action; classical Persian often uses the bare past form with compound subjects. The sense is that both individuals fell asleep.
segments.s288-g20: به سر is a locative 'by/at the head' (indicating position near their heads). بر here reinforces location 'on/upon', while همی is the archaic adverbial particle (here 'thus/still' used before the verb). پاسبانیش (پاسبانی + ش) is 'his/their guarding/watch' as the object, and کرد is the past verb 'did/made' — together: 'he placed himself at their head and kept watch'.
segments.s288-g21: برآمد is the past 'arose' or 'came up'. چو introduces the temporal clause 'when', followed by شب تیره شد 'the night became dark' (تیره 'dark' with شد 'became'). ماهتاب (moonlight) appears after the clause as the rising subject: 'the moonlight rose when the night turned dark.'
segments.s288-g22: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying غمگین سر 'sorrowful heads' (a compact way to say 'the two sorrowful ones'). سر اندر نهاده is a light-verbal idiom from نهادن 'to place' meaning 'laid (their) heads down', and به خواب 'into sleep' marks the goal — i.e. 'the two, sorrowful, put their heads down to sleep.'
segments.s288-g23: رسید is the past 'arrived/reached'. اندر آن جایگه is a locative phrase 'in/at that place'. گستهم is a first-person past form 'I wandered/roamed' appended by the poet as a parenthetical narrator's note — a classical mixture of narrative voices, so here گستهم indicates the speaker's movement or wandering at that time.
segments.s288-g24: که is the relative conjunction 'where/that'. بودند is the past-plural 'they were'. یاران توران is 'the Turanian companions' (یاران = companions, توران = Turan), and به هم is an adverbial phrase 'together' — 'where the Turanian companions were together.'

sentences.s288-s17: The wise man told this great tale, that a lion which flees from the wolf's claw...
sentences.s288-s18: The wolf must not pursue from behind him, for that very fate of his will take him.
sentences.s288-s19: Whatever means he joined and for a while hastened, no one escaped an evil day.
sentences.s288-s20: Quick—hasten! For the army came, from Iran and they took the road toward us.
sentences.s288-s21: Both horsemen sat down; the swift ones drew away from that meadow.
sentences.s288-s22: They turned their faces upward out of the thicket — two blood-stained, valiant, two quarrelsome.
sentences.s288-s23: Both riders rode out onto the plain, anxious and pensive about how many deeds there were to do.
sentences.s288-s24: He appeared from afar; then I (the speaker) moved/turned — they did not see any rider with him.
sentences.s288-s1: When Lahhak and Farshidvard came into view from afar, the company moved off, leaving the plain of battle.
sentences.s288-s2: They traveled for the span of one hour on the seven‑farsang road, safe from the army of Iran.
sentences.s288-s3: They saw a certain grove and a running stream, and into its shade the caravan moved.
sentences.s288-s4: Within the grove were birds, game, and lions; trees all around, green grass, and water below.
sentences.s288-s25: When the brave raised their heads, on seeing him they recognized him.
sentences.s288-s26: They began talking with one another, saying that one man had turned his face toward us.
sentences.s288-s27: He will find no escape from us, unless ill fortune should do him injustice.
sentences.s288-s28: No one will come to the fight except Gostaham; the brave have seized the banner in their grasp.
sentences.s288-s45: He came, bent inward into the saddle; he rode on, and blood kept flowing.
sentences.s288-s46: And from there he came toward a spring; he saw both running water and shade.
sentences.s288-s47: He alighted and (having) tied his horse to a tree, by good fortune he entered the water.
sentences.s288-s48: He drank much water and cried 'Afarin!'; he bound him — you say — throughout the land.
sentences.s288-s53: The Turanian army took the heads of the famed men and carried them before the awakened king.
sentences.s288-s54: Know this so that he may know: I will not die except for fame; in the world, this is the very desire.
sentences.s288-s33: An arrow struck Gostaham on the head, so that his brains were mingled with blood.
sentences.s288-s34: He fell and at the same instant gave up his life; that famed scion of Viseh's race was gone.
sentences.s288-s35: When Lahhak saw his brother's face, he realized that he had ceased fighting (i.e. had died in battle).
sentences.s288-s36: He trembled, and from the pain became dazed; the world before his eyes darkened.
sentences.s288-s5: They descended to hunt, and driven by that thirst they went toward the river.
sentences.s288-s6: When the water came in, bread was needed; whether in sorrow or in joy the mouth should not be closed.
sentences.s288-s7: They returned around that meadow and cast down a great store of prey.
sentences.s288-s8: They kindled a fire and, from that, ate the kebab; then they turned their heads toward sleep.
sentences.s288-s37: His bright spirit reached satiety; he strung the bow with its string and drew it.
sentences.s288-s38: At that moment both riders became weary; with swords they raised battle.
sentences.s288-s39: One by one they reached toward him with outstretched hands; from hatred, so weary, he rushed in.
sentences.s288-s40: A sharp sword struck him on the neck; suddenly from it an uprising arose.
sentences.s288-s13: Navand, his horse, caught the scent of horses, gave a cry and snorted.
sentences.s288-s14: Even Lahak’s nimble horse, at that sign, let out a cry as if bereft of them.
sentences.s288-s15: At once he turned toward Lahak Farshidvard and woke him from his pleasant sleep.
sentences.s288-s16: He said to him, “Get up from this pleasant sleep; seize your fate like a man.”
sentences.s288-s29: One must flee from his presence, unless he turns his face toward this plain.
sentences.s288-s30: From there they turned toward the Hamun; then, in the foothill, stood Gostaham the vengeance-seeker.
sentences.s288-s31: He came; when he drew near them, like a living lion he let out a cry.
sentences.s288-s32: A dagger-like arrow fell upon them; when Farshidvord entered the fight.
sentences.s288-s49: He writhed and tumbled onto the dark earth; his whole body was gashed all over by the sword.
sentences.s288-s50: "He kept saying, 'O shining Creator, bring forth that renowned host.'"
sentences.s288-s51: Out of Bijan's compassion, Giv (was chosen); otherwise, a certain brave man — Niyu — (would have been).
sentences.s288-s52: Whether dead or alive, from this place the host would carry me toward Iran.
sentences.s288-s41: His head fell beneath the foot like a ball that the blow of the polo-mallet keeps striking.
sentences.s288-s42: Such is the conduct of the revolving heaven: it takes away affection from those it has nurtured.
sentences.s288-s43: If you seek his head, you will first find his foot; but if you seek the foot, his head lies before you.
sentences.s288-s44: Upon the saddle I rode so exhausted and ill that you would say it would split apart.
sentences.s288-s9: When ill fortune makes the brave sullen, how can oppression grant them sleep?
sentences.s288-s10: Lahak and Farshidvard sank into deep sleep, and Farshidvard set himself to keep watch at their head.
sentences.s288-s11: When the night grew dark the moonlight rose; the two, sorrowful, laid their heads and went to sleep.
sentences.s288-s12: I arrived in that place, wandering, where the Turanian companions were gathered together.
